parts.text: "Project @nem/ does not compile due to a breaking dependency update. Your goal is to generate a GENERIC, REUSABLE transformation rule - not a one-off patch - that can be applied to ANY Maven project affected by the same breaking change. Plan: 1) Identify the compilation errors in @nem/ and determine which API    (class/method/constructor/signature) changed in the dependency.    The new dependency API specification is available at /workspace/flyway-core-9.15.0-api.md and the Javadoc at /workspace/javadoc-flyway-core-9.15.0/    for reference when identifying replacement types, methods, or fields. 2) Characterize the breaking change abstractly:    - What was the old API pattern? (e.g., Foo.bar(String))    - What is the new API pattern? (e.g., Foo.bar(String, boolean))    - What structural transformation does this require at the call site?    Do NOT assume the fix is specific to this project - any project calling the old API will need the same transformation. 3) Generate a GENERIC Spoon transformation using the template in @/workspace/spoon-base-template/:    - Match the old API pattern structurally, not by project-specific class names.    - Use patterns to traverse all files and apply the fix wherever the old pattern appears.    - Parameterize by fully-qualified type names and method signatures from the dependency, NOT from the client.    - Save the transformation in /workspace/spoon-base-template/src/main/java/github/chains/Main.java.    - Use the Spoon API documentation located in folder /workspace/spoon-api-docs/ for reference. 4) Compile the transformation and verify it has no errors. 5) Apply the transformation to @nem/ and ensure both compilation errors and test errors are fixed. 6) Ensure generalizability: ensure the generated rule contains no hardcoded project-specific identifiers.    The rule must be applicable to other projects by simply changing the input source directory path."

**Evaluating source files repair**

I think the files in nem/nis/src/main/java need to be repaired since the prettyprint converted all the imports incorrectly. We need to restore the proper import lines, and the best way seems to be using git checkout or restore to revert the changes. However, the developer instructions caution against using destructive commands unless approved. I wonder if git restore is considered destructive. Perhaps I should check with the user, but they want the task completed. A non-interactive git restore might be a solution!

**Considering source file restoration**

I’m thinking about an alternative where I run the transformer on a copy of the source directory in /tmp and then apply a patch to the target file. The original source tree would still be damaged, so I need to figure out if any files were actually modified during this run. Probably the best move is to restore the entire tree to HEAD since the user hasn’t specified otherwise. I should check the git status first to confirm.

The rewrite pass damaged import statements by re-prettyprinting the whole source tree with incomplete resolution. I’m checking the tracked changes so I can restore the affected files cleanly and rerun the transformation in a safer way.
